ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed bun class; no specific coconut lotus seed paste variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJL covers steamed bread/bun category; no coconut lotus filling sub-code exists. Approximate match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for coconut lotus seed paste steamed bun. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is 3 steamed buns weighing approximately 180 g in total, as commonly served in Singapore dim sum restaurants and hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo Database): Lotus Paste Bun (Steamed) and Coconut-filled Steamed Bun analogues
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Deluxe Coconut Lotus Bun' exists in the HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB entries for lotus paste steamed bun and coconut-filled steamed bun, weighted to reflect the coconut-lotus filling composition. Input per_100g values (energy 250 kcal, protein 5.56 g, fat 8.33 g, carbohydrate 36.11 g) are consistent with HPB analogues and are retained with medium confidence. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Bread, white, commercially prepared' (FDC ID 325871) used as secondary cross-check for dough macros.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Steamed white bread / bun analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for coconut lotus seed paste steamed bun. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed white bread and filled steamed buns (GI range 55–70). The refined wheat flour dough is the dominant carbohydrate vehicle; the sweetened lotus seed paste filling raises the sugar fraction but does not substantially lower GI. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 36.11 × 1.80) / 100 = 65 × 65.0 g / 100 ≈ 42.4. Serving = 180 g; available carbohydrate per serving = 36.11 × 1.80 = 65.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (13.89 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.67 g/100 g) triggers insulin-mediated sodium retention despite relatively low sodium (138.89 mg/100 g); saturated fat from coconut milk (4.44 g/100 g) contributes to endothelial stress.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to one bun per occasion and avoid pairing with other high-sugar or high-sodium foods, as the elevated sugar load promotes insulin-driven fluid retention that can elevate blood pressure independent of dietary sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (36.11 g/100 g) with high sugar (13.89 g/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.67 g/100 g) produces a high glycaemic load (GL ≈ 42 per 3-bun serving), causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; estimated GI of 65 (medium-high for a steamed refined wheat product).
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this food to half a bun per occasion, monitor postprandial glucose closely, and prefer wholegrain or reduced-sugar alternatives, as the high glycaemic load of a standard 3-bun serving substantially exceeds recommended single-meal carbohydrate thresholds.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (4.44 g/100 g, approximately 8.0 g per 3-bun serving) and trans fat (0.06 g/100 g) contribute to LDL-raising lipid intake; high sugar (13.89 g/100 g) promotes triglyceride synthesis and low-grade inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to one bun occasionally, as the saturated fat from coconut milk and the high sugar content together elevate LDL cholesterol and triglycerides, both established cardiovascular risk factors per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Coconut-derived saturated fat (4.44 g/100 g) does not qualify for the MUFA/PUFA anti-inflammatory carve-out; sugar at 13.89 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory mediator production; no qualifying anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, or fibre >3 g/100 g) are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise consumption of this bun as the combination of coconut saturated fat and high sugar content may exacerbate systemic inflammation without any offsetting anti-inflammatory nutritional benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coconut Lotus Seed Paste Steamed Bun (椰汁莲蓉包, Ye Zhi Lian Rong Bao) is a Chinese dim sum item popular in Singapore hawker centres and restaurants. The bun consists of a soft, fluffy steamed white dough made from refined wheat flour, yeast, sugar, and a small amount of vegetable shortening, encasing a sweet filling of lotus seed paste blended with coconut milk. A standard serving is three buns weighing approximately 180 g. The exterior is smooth and white with a slightly springy texture; the interior filling is dense, fragrant, and moderately sweet with a distinctive coconut aroma. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides approximately 250 kcal, 5.56 g protein, 8.33 g fat (of which 4.44 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 36.11 g carbohydrate with 13.89 g sugar and only 1.67 g dietary fibre. The high refined carbohydrate and sugar content with low fibre produces a high glycaemic load (estimated GL ≈ 42 per 3-bun serving, GI ≈ 65), making this food unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The saturated fat from coconut milk raises cardiovascular concern. This dish is vegetarian and vegan-friendly, contains gluten, and is free of dairy and cholesterol. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast item, dim sum brunch dish, or snack in Chinese-Singaporean culinary culture.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lotus bun?
Can diabetics eat lotus paste bun?
How much sugar in a lotus bun?
Is coconut lotus bun suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Deluxe Coconut Lotus Bun' exists in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB analogues for lotus paste steamed bun and coconut-filled steamed bun, which are consistent with the input per_100g values. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database steamed bun entries. Medium confidence is appropriate as the values are plausible and source-anchored but not from an exact match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for coconut lotus seed paste steamed bun is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo analogues used for this record and are not available for this specific dish in any approved source as of 2024. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): the bun contains no animal-derived ingredients; lotus seed paste and coconut milk are plant-based, and cholesterol is absent by ingredient composition, consistent with HPB data for plant-based steamed buns. 4. vitamin_c_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): no vitamin C-contributing ingredients are present in this formulation; confirmed consistent with HPB data for steamed buns. 5. trans_fat_g of 0.06 g/100 g is retained from input; this trace level is plausible from vegetable shortening used in commercial bun production. 6. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database steamed white bread/bun entries (GI range 55–70); no direct GI measurement for coconut lotus bun was found. 7. Input source URL 'https://foodo.sg/' corrected to canonical HPB Foodo URL 'https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/' as the approved source. 8. FoodEx2 code A0EJL used as approximate match for steamed bread/bun category; no filling-specific sub-code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
